_source: ACI (American Concrete Institute) standards, Masonry codes, NCMA Technical Manual, Trade practice guides
_note: Long-tail masonry knowledge. Concrete mixing and finishing, block/brick laying, mortar types, reinforcement, expansion joints, waterproofing.

portland_cement_types.type_i: General purpose; most common; moderate sulfate resistance; standard setting time 2-4 hours
portland_cement_types.type_ii: Moderate sulfate resistance; slower heat generation; mass concrete, foundations; setting time 3-5 hours
portland_cement_types.type_iii: High early strength; cold weather; fast track construction; 7-day strength in 3 days; higher heat generation
portland_cement_types.type_iv: Low heat; mass concrete (dams); very slow strength gain; rarely used residentially
portland_cement_types.type_v: High sulfate resistance; coastal areas, sewer systems, high groundwater sulfate; specialized applications

concrete_mix_design.water_cement_ratio: Critical for strength; 0.4-0.6 typical; lower ratio = higher strength but less workable; 0.45 gives ~4000 PSI at 28 days
concrete_mix_design.aggregate_gradation: Well-graded (various sizes) gives denser, stronger concrete; 60-75% of mix volume; coarse (>1/4") and fine (sand)

admixtures.water_reducers: Plasticizers; maintain workability with less water; increase strength 10-15%; retard or accelerate set time
admixtures.air_entrainers: Creates tiny air bubbles; freeze-thaw resistance; required in cold climates; 4-8% air content; reduces strength slightly
admixtures.accelerators: Calcium chloride typical; faster setting/strength gain; cold weather; CANNOT use with reinforcing steel (corrosion)
admixtures.retarders: Slow setting time; hot weather; large pours; allows finishing time; sugar-based typically

concrete_mix_design.slump: Measure of consistency/workability; 3-4 inch typical for formed work; 5-6 inch for slabs; 6-8 inch with superplasticizer; too high = segregation
concrete_mix_design.psi_strength: 2500 PSI residential slabs, 3000-4000 PSI driveways/structural, 4000-5000 PSI commercial, 5000+ PSI high-performance; specified at 28 days

proportions_by_weight.1_2_3_rule: Simplified: 1 part cement, 2 parts sand, 3 parts gravel; residential rule of thumb; add water to 0.5 ratio; not for engineered work
proportions_by_weight.5_bag_mix: 5 bags cement per cubic yard; moderate strength ~3000 PSI; common residential
proportions_by_weight.6_bag_mix: 6 bags per cubic yard; higher strength ~4000 PSI; driveways, commercial
proportions_by_weight.calculating_cement: Cubic yards × bags per yard = total bags; 1 bag = 94 lbs = 1 cubic foot; 27 cubic feet per cubic yard

subgrade_preparation.compaction: 95% compaction minimum; plate compactor or jumping jack; prevents settlement and cracking; test with Proctor test
subgrade_preparation.base_material: 4-6 inches crushed stone (3/4" minus); allows drainage; stable platform; compact in lifts (2-3 inches)
subgrade_preparation.vapor_barrier: 6 mil polyethylene under slab; prevents moisture from below; overlap seams 6 inches; protect from punctures
subgrade_preparation.formwork: 2×4 or 2×6 for residential; 2×8+ for thicker slabs; stake every 3-4 feet; level and straight; grease or oil for release

reinforcement.rebar: #3 (3/8"), #4 (1/2"), #5 (5/8") common; grid pattern 12-18 inches on center; support on chairs (dobies) to maintain 2-3 inch cover
reinforcement.wire_mesh: 6×6 W1.4×W1.4 (10 gauge) common residential; placed mid-slab (not on ground); overlap panels 6 inches; controls cracking but doesn't prevent
reinforcement.fiber_reinforcement: Synthetic or steel fibers; replaces wire mesh for residential; 1.5 lbs per cubic yard typical; controls shrinkage cracks; mixes in concrete
reinforcement.control_joints: Saw-cut or tooled grooves; 1/4 slab depth; space at 2-3× slab thickness in feet (4" slab = 8-12 ft spacing); creates weak point for crack to form

pouring_procedure.placement: Start far corner; work backward toward exit; pour in layers <24 inches if deep; consolidate each layer before next
pouring_procedure.consolidation: Vibrate to remove air pockets; internal vibrator (stinger) for walls/columns; don't over-vibrate (segregation); vibrate at 18-24 inch intervals
pouring_procedure.screeding: Strike off excess concrete; 2×4 or magnesium screed; sawing motion; brings cream (fines) to surface
pouring_procedure.floating: Bull float for large areas; hand float small areas; levels high spots, fills low spots; brings cream up for finishing; immediately after screeding
pouring_procedure.edging: Round edges with edger tool; prevents chipping; run along forms; do after floating, before troweling
pouring_procedure.jointing: Groover (jointer) tool for control joints; 1/4 depth; straightedge guide; do after edging, before troweling

finishing.troweling: Steel trowel; 2-4 passes; each pass increases smoothness and density; wait for bleed water to evaporate before troweling (shine disappears)
finishing.broom_finish: Drag broom across surface; slip resistance; driveways, sidewalks, ramps; do as final step after troweling
finishing.exposed_aggregate: Seed aggregate into surface or wash off cream; slip resistant; decorative; use retarder on surface, wash after initial set
finishing.stamped_concrete: Stamp pattern (stone, brick) into surface; color with integral color or hardener; seal after curing; requires skill and speed
finishing.timing: Too early = mar surface, tear cream; too late = difficult to work, weak surface; watch for shine to disappear (bleed water gone)

curing_methods.water_curing: Keep wet 7 days; spray, pond, or cover with wet burlap; best method for strength; labor intensive
curing_methods.curing_compound: Spray-on membrane; seals in moisture; apply immediately after finishing; wax or resin-based; economical; 200-400 sq ft per gallon
curing_methods.plastic_sheeting: Cover slab with plastic; seal edges with dirt/weights; moisture stays in; economical; can leave marks if applied too soon
curing_methods.wet_burlap: Cover with burlap, keep wet; prevents rapid drying; good for columns/walls; labor to re-wet frequently

importance_of_curing.strength_gain: Concrete gains 70% strength in 7 days, 90% in 28 days, continues years; inadequate curing = 50% strength loss
importance_of_curing.shrinkage_cracks: Proper curing reduces shrinkage cracking; concrete shrinks ~0.05% (6 inches per 100 feet); rapid drying = surface cracks
importance_of_curing.durability: Proper curing increases freeze-thaw resistance, abrasion resistance, impermeability; affects surface hardness

cold_weather_concrete.definition: Air temp <40°F or concrete temp <50°F for >3 consecutive hours
cold_weather_concrete.precautions: Heat water and/or aggregates; use Type III cement or accelerator (non-chloride if reinforced); protect from freezing 48-72 hours; insulated blankets or heated enclosures
cold_weather_concrete.minimum_temp: Maintain concrete >50°F for 3-7 days; never let freeze before reaching 500 PSI (~24-48 hours); frozen water expands, damages concrete
cold_weather_concrete.do_not: Do NOT use salt-based accelerators with rebar (corrosion); do NOT pour on frozen ground (thaw first or insulate)

hot_weather_concrete.definition: Air temp >85°F or concrete temp >85°F; rapid evaporation, fast setting
hot_weather_concrete.precautions: Cool aggregates (shade, spray with water); use ice as part of mix water; use retarder; shade forms; wet subgrade; start early morning or evening
hot_weather_concrete.placement: Work quickly; have adequate crew; minimize time between batching and placement (<90 min); continuous pour for large areas
hot_weather_concrete.curing: Critical; start immediately after finishing; fog spray or curing compound; plastic sheeting; prevent rapid moisture loss

concrete_block_cmu.sizes: 8×8×16 most common (nominal, actual 7-5/8×7-5/8×15-5/8); 6×8×16 and 12×8×16 also; 3/8" mortar joint brings to nominal size
concrete_block_cmu.types: Standard (hollow), solid, bond beam (U-shaped for rebar/grout), half block, corner block, lintel block
concrete_block_cmu.weight: Lightweight (22-28 lbs, pumice/expanded shale aggregate), medium (35-40 lbs), heavyweight (50+ lbs, dense aggregate); lightweight easier to handle
concrete_block_cmu.strength: 1900-3000 PSI compressive strength typical; higher for structural; check specs for load-bearing vs partition

types.common_brick: Building brick; no special finish; structural; covered with stucco or siding; economical
types.face_brick: Finished appearance; color consistency; exterior visible; higher cost; FBX (select), FBS (standard), FBA (architectural)
types.fire_brick: Refractory; high heat resistance; fireplaces, chimneys, kilns; withstands 2000°F+
types.paving_brick: High compressive strength; abrasion resistant; driveways, patios, streets

brick.dimensions: Modular 3-5/8×2-1/4×7-5/8 (nominal 4×2-2/3×8 with mortar); Standard 3-5/8×2-1/4×8; three courses = 8 inches with 3/8" joints
brick.grades: Grade SW (severe weather, freeze-thaw), Grade MW (moderate weather), Grade NW (no weather, interior); SW for exterior in freezing climates

stone.types: Granite (hardest, durable, expensive), limestone (softer, easy to cut, moderate), sandstone (porous, varies), slate (layered, roof/floors), marble (beautiful, soft, stains)
stone.veneer_vs_structural: Veneer (thin <4", facing only, tied to backup wall), Full-bed/Structural (thick, load-bearing); most residential is veneer
stone.finishes: Rough (natural face), split-face (cleft), honed (smooth matte), polished (glossy); affects slip resistance and appearance

mortar_types.type_n: General purpose; above grade; interior/exterior; low strength 750 PSI; residential veneer; 1:1:6 ratio (cement:lime:sand)
mortar_types.type_s: High strength 1800 PSI; below grade; retaining walls; structural; 1:0.5:4.5 ratio; good bond, flexural strength
mortar_types.type_m: Highest strength 2500 PSI; heavy load, below grade; severe freeze-thaw; 1:0.25:3 ratio; rigid, less workable
mortar_types.type_o: Low strength 350 PSI; interior; non-load bearing; repointing historic (matches old mortar); 1:2:9 ratio; very soft
mortar_types.selection: Stronger wall = weaker mortar (allows movement); Type N residential, Type S structural/below grade, Type M heavy duty/commercial

mixing.proportions: By volume; measure with shovel or bucket; consistency more important than exact ratio; adjust for conditions
mixing.water_content: Add gradually; workable but not soupy; should hang on trowel at 90°; too wet = weak, too dry = poor bond
mixing.mixing_time: 3-5 minutes in mixer; hand mixing: dry mix first, add water, mix thoroughly; don't over-mix (reduces workability)
mixing.retempering: Add small amount water if stiffens during use; only within 2-1/2 hours of mixing; discard after (cement hydration started)

mortar_joints.concave: Rounded inward; best water resistance; most common; use jointer tool; compacts mortar
mortar_joints.v_joint: V-shaped; decorative; good water resistance; use V-jointer
mortar_joints.flush: Cut flush with wall; rustic appearance; poor water resistance; easy
mortar_joints.raked: Recessed; shadow lines; very poor water resistance; interior only or dry climates; not for freezing climates
mortar_joints.struck: Angled; traditional; moderate water resistance; slopes outward at top (bad for water)
mortar_joints.timing: Tool when thumbprint hard; typically 30-60 min after laying; too early = smears, too late = dark streaks

block_laying.preparation: Snap chalk lines for layout; dry lay first course (no mortar) to check spacing; adjust for openings (doors, windows)
block_laying.mortar_bed: Face shell bedding (edges only, not webs) for standard block; full bed for first course on footing; 3/8-1/2 inch joint
block_laying.laying_sequence: Corners first (lead); build up 4-5 courses; check level and plumb frequently; string line between corners for intermediate blocks
block_laying.butter_and_place: Butter end of block (standing on end); place firmly, tap with trowel handle; squeeze mortar out; tool joint when thumbprint hard
block_laying.level_plumb_straight: Check each block: level (bubble level), plumb (4-ft level vertical), straight (string line); adjust immediately, tapping or adding mortar
block_laying.cutting_blocks: Score with brick set (chisel), strike to break; or use masonry saw (diamond blade); wet cut reduces dust; wear respirator if dry cutting

patterns.running_bond: Offset 1/2 brick; most common; horizontal lines; each course shifts half brick; stagger vertical joints
patterns.stack_bond: Vertical lines; modern look; weak (no interlock); requires horizontal reinforcement
patterns.flemish_bond: Alternating headers (short end) and stretchers (long side); decorative; structural interlock; traditional
patterns.english_bond: Alternating courses of all headers and all stretchers; strong; traditional

brick_laying.technique: Mortar bed on previous course; butter one end of brick; push into place; tap level with trowel handle; maintain 3/8" joints; check string line
brick_laying.closures: Last brick in course; butter both ends; slide into place; may need to cut for fit
brick_laying.wythe: Single width of brick; multi-wythe walls (cavity or solid); tie wythes with headers or metal ties

grout_reinforcement.grout: Fluid concrete; fills block cells; bonds with rebar; higher slump 8-11 inches; pea gravel aggregate; pour in 4-5 ft lifts
grout_reinforcement.grouting_procedure: Consolidate with rod or vibrator; reconsolidate after water absorption (30 min); don't over-vibrate (segregation); grout same day as top course
grout_reinforcement.rebar_placement: Vertical bars in cells (every 4 ft or per engineer); horizontal in bond beam courses; lap 40× diameter (40× 1/2" = 20" lap); chairs to hold position
grout_reinforcement.bond_beam: Horizontal reinforcement; U-block or knocked out webs; rebar runs continuous; at top of wall, openings, mid-height per code
grout_reinforcement.inspection: Special inspection often required for grouted/reinforced masonry; inspector verifies rebar placement before grout; tests grout samples

footings.sizing: Width 2× wall thickness minimum; 16" wide for 8" wall typical; depth below frost line (18-48" depending on climate); bearing pressure on soil ~1500-2000 PSF
footings.reinforcement: 2-#4 rebar continuous in footing; 3" cover bottom, 2" ends; dowels into wall every 4 ft; size per engineer for heavy loads
footings.stepped_footings: On slopes; step max 2 ft; overlap steps by thickness of footing or 2 ft (whichever greater); rebar continuous through steps

lintels.purpose: Span opening (door, window); carry load above; transfer to sides
lintels.steel_angle: Most common; L-shaped; size depends on span and load; 3-1/2×3-1/2×1/4" for 4 ft span typical; bear 8" minimum each end
lintels.precast_concrete: U-shaped lintel block with rebar and grout; or solid precast lintel; architectural (matches block); bear 8" minimum
lintels.sizing: 8-10" bearing each end; length = opening + 16-20"; engineering required for heavy loads or long spans (>8 ft)

control_joints.purpose: Relieve stress from shrinkage, thermal movement, settlement; weak plane for crack to form (controlled crack)
control_joints.spacing: Concrete block: 25-30 ft on center, at openings, change in wall height, intersections; Brick: 20-25 ft
control_joints.construction: Rake out mortar; backer rod; sealant; or special control joint units (no bond between units); continuous from top to footing
control_joints.movement_joints: Include control joints (in-plane), expansion joints (allow growth), isolation joints (separate elements); don't forget!

flashing_weep_holes.flashing: Through-wall flashing; above openings, at base of wall, shelf angles; direct water out; copper, stainless, rubberized membrane
flashing_weep_holes.placement: Extend through wall; slope to exterior; turn up at back wall 8"; overlap end laps 6"; seal laps
flashing_weep_holes.weep_holes: Vent/drain cavity; rope wick, plastic tube, open head joint (leave out mortar); space 24" o.c.; immediately above flashing
flashing_weep_holes.importance: Water gets in masonry; flashing and weeps let it out; prevents rot, freeze-thaw damage, efflorescence, interior water damage

repointing_tuckpointing.assessment: Deteriorated mortar (soft, crumbling, recessed >1/2"); water infiltration; efflorescence; step cracks (settlement)
repointing_tuckpointing.removal: Grinder with diamond blade (controlled depth), or chisel (by hand, slow); remove 2-3× joint width depth (3/4-1" typical); don't damage masonry
repointing_tuckpointing.cleaning: Blow out dust; wet joint (spray bottle); mortar bonds better to damp surface
repointing_tuckpointing.mortar_matching: Test existing mortar (hardness, color); match or slightly weaker than original; Type N for old brick (soft), Type O for historic
repointing_tuckpointing.application: Pack mortar firmly into joint; fill from back to front; compact in layers; tool to match existing profile; cure slowly (mist)

crack_repair.hairline_cracks: Cosmetic; <1/16"; monitor for growth; seal with masonry sealer or leave; not structural
crack_repair.structural_cracks: >1/4", step pattern, widening, horizontal; indicate settlement, foundation issue, inadequate reinforcement; engineer evaluation required
crack_repair.epoxy_injection: For structural cracks; inject epoxy under pressure; bonds concrete back together; restores strength; surface seal ports, inject from bottom up
crack_repair.routing_sealing: Widen crack (V or U shape), clean, backer rod, flexible sealant (polyurethane, silicone); allows movement; not structural repair

spalling_damage.causes: Freeze-thaw (water in masonry freezes, expands, pops off face); rusting rebar (expands); salt damage; poor quality masonry
spalling_damage.repair: Remove loose material; clean; bonding agent; patch with mortar (thick) or overlay (thin); match color/texture; cure properly
spalling_damage.prevention: Seal masonry (breathable sealer); proper drainage/flashing; avoid salt in freeze-thaw climates; use air-entrained mortar in cold regions

efflorescence.cause: Water-soluble salts deposited on surface as water evaporates; white crystalline powder; from mortar, masonry units, soil, water
efflorescence.removal: Dry brush; or dilute muriatic acid (1:10 with water); wet surface first, apply acid, scrub, rinse thoroughly; wear PPE (gloves, goggles, respirator)
efflorescence.prevention: Reduce water infiltration (flashing, weeps, sealers); use low-alkali cement; cover masonry during construction; proper drainage

essential_tools.trowels: Brick trowel (triangle), pointing trowel (small), margin trowel (small rectangle); butter and place mortar; sizes vary by preference
essential_tools.levels: 4-ft level (plumb, level), 2-ft level (blocks), torpedo level (small areas, tight spaces); check frequently
essential_tools.jointers: Concave jointer (most common), V-jointer, slicker, raker; shape mortar joints; various sizes for different joint widths
essential_tools.cutting_tools: Brick set (chisel) and hammer (score and break), masonry saw (wet or dry), angle grinder with diamond blade
essential_tools.mixing_tools: Mortar mixer (electric/gas), mixing hoe, wheelbarrow or mortar board, shovel, buckets
essential_tools.string_and_line: Mason's line (strong, doesn't stretch), line blocks or pins, chalk line (layout)

safety.ppe: Hard hat, safety glasses (or face shield for grinding), gloves (leather or rubber), steel toe boots, knee pads, dust mask (N95 for dry cutting)
safety.silica_dust: Masonry creates crystalline silica dust (lung disease, silicosis, cancer); wet cutting, local exhaust ventilation, respirator, OSHA regulations
safety.lifting: Blocks weigh 25-50 lbs; proper lifting (bend knees, not back), team lift if heavy, mechanical aids (hod, forklift) for bulk
safety.scaffolding: Proper setup (level, tied to structure, guardrails, toe boards); inspect daily; follow OSHA standards; max height 4:1 (height:base width)
safety.alkaline_burns: Wet cement/mortar is caustic (pH ~12); skin contact = burns; wash immediately; eyes = flush 15 min, medical attention; wear gloves

material_quantities.concrete: Length × Width × Depth (ft) / 27 = cubic yards; add 10% waste; 4" slab: 1 sq ft = 0.012 CY; 100 sq ft = 1.2 CY
material_quantities.block: Surface area / block face area + 10% waste; 8×16 block = 1.125 sq ft face (0.89 blocks per sq ft); 100 sq ft wall = 89 + 10% = 98 blocks
material_quantities.brick: Modular brick: 7 per sq ft (with mortar joints); standard brick: 6.5 per sq ft; add 5-10% waste
material_quantities.mortar: 35-40 blocks per 80-lb bag mortar mix; 150 bricks per bag; 30 sq ft stone veneer per bag (varies by joint size)
material_quantities.rebar: Linear feet needed + 40× diameter for laps + 10% waste; ties (wire) every 2 ft; chairs at 4 ft spacing

labor.productivity: Mason lays 100-150 blocks per day (8 hrs), 300-500 bricks per day; varies with skill, complexity, weather, height
labor.crew: 1 mason + 1 tender/laborer typical; tender mixes mortar, brings materials, cleans up; 2:1 or 3:1 ratio for larger crews
labor.time_estimate: Blocks / productivity + 20% for setup, cleanup, breaks; 1000 block wall = 7-10 days for single mason + tender
